subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : If dQ and dW represent the heat supplied to the system and the work done on the system respectively. Then according to the first law of thermodynamics dQ=dU-dW.

Reason R : First law of thermodynamics is based on law of conservation of energy.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : If dQ and dW represent the heat supplied to the system and the work done on the system respectively. Then according to the first law of thermodynamics dQ=dU-dW.

Reason R : First law of thermodynamics is based on law of conservation of energy.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below :
options: A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
solution:
The first law of thermodynamics is stated as dQ=dU-dW. Since dQ is heat supplied to the system and positive thus, dW is work done on the system, i.e., negative work.

We know that the first law of thermodynamics deals with work done, and heat energy supplied or removed from a system. Therefore, it is based on the conservation of energy. The  law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another.

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: An inventor claims to have constructed an engine that has an efficiency of $30 \%$ when operated between the boiling and freezing points of water. This is not possible.
Statement 2: The efficiency of a real engine is always less than the efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between the same two temperatures.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: An inventor claims to have constructed an engine that has an efficiency of $30 \%$ when operated between the boiling and freezing points of water. This is not possible.
Statement 2: The efficiency of a real engine is always less than the efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between the same two temperatures.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
According to Carnot's theorem - no heat engine working between two given temperatures of source and sink can be more efficient than a perfectly reversible engine i.e. Carnot engine working between the same two temperatures.
Efficiency of Carnot's engine, $n=1-\frac{T_2}{T_1}$ where, $T_1=$ temperature of source $T_2=$ temperature of sink

q: In 1st  case, Carnot engine operates between temperatures 300 K and 100 K. In 2nd  case, as shown in the figure, a combination of two engines is used. The efficiency of this combination (in 2nd  case) will be :

solution:
The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between two temperatures is given by η=1-TLTH

For the first case : η=1-100300=23

For the second case : ηnet =η1+η2-η1η2

η1=1-200300=13

η2=1-100200=12

ηnet =13+12-16=23

η(first case)=η(second case)

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the characteristics of an adiabatic process in a monoatomic gas.
(A) Internal energy is constant.
(B) Work done in the process is equal to the charge in internal energy.
(C) The product of temperature and volume is a constant.
(D) The product of pressure and volume is a constant.
(E) The work done to change the temperature from $\mathrm{T}_1$ to $\mathrm{T}_2$ is proportional to $\left(\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the characteristics of an adiabatic process in a monoatomic gas.
(A) Internal energy is constant.
(B) Work done in the process is equal to the charge in internal energy.
(C) The product of temperature and volume is a constant.
(D) The product of pressure and volume is a constant.
(E) The work done to change the temperature from $\mathrm{T}_1$ to $\mathrm{T}_2$ is proportional to $\left(\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W}=0 \Rightarrow-\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{W} \\ & \mathrm{WD}=-\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{WD}|=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \& \mathrm{E}$ [Only possibility]

subject: Physics
q: A diatomic gas $(\gamma=1.4)$ does 100 J of work when it is expanded isobarically. Then the heat given to the gas $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: A diatomic gas $(\gamma=1.4)$ does 100 J of work when it is expanded isobarically. Then the heat given to the gas $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
For an isobaric process, the first law gives: $Q = \Delta U + W$.
For a diatomic gas with $\gamma = 1.4$, we have $C_V = \frac{5}{2}R$ and $C_P = \frac{7}{2}R$.
Since $W = nR\Delta T = 100$ J for isobaric expansion, the change in internal energy is: $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T = \frac{5}{2}nR\Delta T = \frac{5}{2} \times 100 = 250$ J.
Therefore, $Q = 250 + 100 = 350$ J.

question: Which of the following is incorrect regarding the first law of thermodynamics?
options: It is applicable to any cyclic process
It is a restatement of the principle of conservation of energy
It introduces the concept of the internal energy
It introduces the concept of the entropy
solution: The first law of thermodynamics states the principle of conservation of energy. It introduces the concept of internal energy. And it is applicable for any cycle.

q: n moles of an ideal gas undergoes a process A→B as shown in the figure. The maximum temperature of the gas during the process will be:
question: n moles of an ideal gas undergoes a process A→B as shown in the figure. The maximum temperature of the gas during the process will be:
solution:
Temperature T will be maximum where product of PV is max equation of line

P=−P0V0V+3P0

Multiplying both sides by V,

PV=−P0V0V2+3P0V

For maxima,            d(PV) dV =0

⇒               −2 P 0 V 0 V+3 P 0 =0     ⇒  V= 3 V 0 2

and P=3P02

⇒   T= PV nR = 9 P 0 V 0 4 nR

Alternate method :

Since initial and final temperature are equal.

Hence maximum temperature is at middle of line.

PV=nR T max

⇒Tmax=3P023V02nR=9P0V04nR

The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
question: Work done by a Carnot engine operating between temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>127</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi></math>. The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
solution:
The temperatures in Kelvin scales are 

T2=27 °C+273=300 KT1=127 °C+273=400 K

The efficiency of the Carnot engine is

 η=1-T2T1=1-300400=14
Using the relation, η=WQ
⇒2 kJQ=14⇒Q=8 kJ

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following statements:
A. Zeroth law of thermodynamics gives concept of temperature
B. First law of thermodynamics gives concept of internal energy
C. In isothermal expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$
D. Product of intensive and extensive variables is extensive
E. The ratio of any extensive variable to mass will be an extensive variable
Choose the correct combination of statements from the options given below:
question:
Consider the following statements:
A. Zeroth law of thermodynamics gives concept of temperature
B. First law of thermodynamics gives concept of internal energy
C. In isothermal expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$
D. Product of intensive and extensive variables is extensive
E. The ratio of any extensive variable to mass will be an extensive variable
Choose the correct combination of statements from the options given below:
solution:
Statement A is correct because the zeroth law of thermodynamics defines temperature and thermal equilibrium.

Statement B is correct because the first law of thermodynamics introduces the concept of internal energy through the equation $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$.

Statement C is incorrect because for an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the change in internal energy $\Delta U = 0$. According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta W$.

Statement D is correct because if an intensive variable (which is independent of the size of the system) is multiplied by an extensive variable (which is directly proportional to the size of the system), the product will be directly proportional to the size of the system, making it an extensive variable.

Statement E is incorrect because the ratio of two extensive variables (like any extensive variable and mass) is independent of the size of the system, making it an intensive variable.

Therefore, only statements A, B, and D are correct.

Answer: A, B and D Only

subject: Physics
q:
Density of water at $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and $998 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ respectively. The increase in internal energy of 4 kg of water when it is heated from $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
(specific heat capacity of water $=4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$. and 1 atmospheric pressure $=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$)
question:
Density of water at $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and $998 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ respectively. The increase in internal energy of 4 kg of water when it is heated from $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
(specific heat capacity of water $=4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$. and 1 atmospheric pressure $=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$)
solution:
Heat absorbed by water: $Q = mc\Delta T = 4 \times 4200 \times 16 = 268800$ J.
Volume change: $V_1 = 4/1000 = 0.004$ m³ at 4°C, $V_2 = 4/998 ≈ 0.00401$ m³ at 20°C.
Work done by water at constant pressure: $W = P_{atm}\Delta V = 10^5 \times 0.00001 = 0.8$ J.
By first law of thermodynamics: $\Delta U = Q - W = 268800 - 0.8 = 268799.2$ J

14=1-TLTH
TLTH=34
2η=12=1-TL-58TH

2 TL=3 TL-174
TL=174 K

subject: Physics
q: In an Isothermal change, the change in pressure and volume of a gas can be represented for three different temperature; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> as:
question: In an Isothermal change, the change in pressure and volume of a gas can be represented for three different temperature; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> as:
solution:
In case of isothermal process, pressure vs volume graph is a rectangular hyperbola.

The graph of the isothermal process shifts away from the origin as the temperature of the process is increased. Thus, the curve away from the origin T3 have the highest temperature whereas the curve closer to the origin T1 have the lowest temperature.

Here, the dotted line shows the isobaric line that implies T3>T2>T1 as T=PVnR.

subject: Physics
q: A certain amount of gas of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> temperature and pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> expands isothermally until its volume gets doubled. Later it expands adiabatically until its volume gets redoubled. The final pressure of the gas will be (Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>)
question: A certain amount of gas of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> temperature and pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> expands isothermally until its volume gets doubled. Later it expands adiabatically until its volume gets redoubled. The final pressure of the gas will be (Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>)
solution:
Given here, P1=2×107 N m-2=2×107 Pa

Using Boyle's law,

P1V1=P2V2

Since V2=2V1.

Hence, P2=P12 (isothermal expansion)

P2=1×107 Pa

Now, during adiabatic expansion, 

P2V2γ=P32V2γ

P3=1×10721.5=3.536×106 Pa

subject: Physics
q: The door of a working refrigerator is left open in a well insulated room. The temperature of air in the room will
question: The door of a working refrigerator is left open in a well insulated room. The temperature of air in the room will
options: increase in winters and decrease in summers
solution: In a refrigerator, the heat dissipated in the atmosphere is more than that taken from the cooling chamber, therefore the room is heated. If the door of a refrigerator is kept open.

subject: Physics
q:
A total of $48 \mathrm{~J}$ heat is given to one mole of helium kept in a cylinder. The temperature of helium increases by $2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The work done by the gas is:
Given, $\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
question:
A total of $48 \mathrm{~J}$ heat is given to one mole of helium kept in a cylinder. The temperature of helium increases by $2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The work done by the gas is:
Given, $\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
solution:
$1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W} \\
& \Rightarrow+48=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}+\mathrm{W} \\
& \Rightarrow 48=(1)\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{2}\right)(2)+\mathrm{W} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{W}=48-3 \times \mathrm{R} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{W}=48-3 \times(8.3) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{W}=23.1 \text { Joule }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The change in the magnitude of the volume of an ideal gas when a small additional pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> is applied at a constant temperature, is the same as the change when the temperature is reduced by a small quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at constant pressure. The initial temperature and pressure of the gas were <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> respectively. If  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>ΔT</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>|</mo><mi>ΔP</mi><mo>|</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>atm</mi><mo>)</mo></math>  is __________
question: The change in the magnitude of the volume of an ideal gas when a small additional pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> is applied at a constant temperature, is the same as the change when the temperature is reduced by a small quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at constant pressure. The initial temperature and pressure of the gas were <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> respectively. If  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>ΔT</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>|</mo><mi>ΔP</mi><mo>|</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>atm</mi><mo>)</mo></math>  is __________
solution:
PV=nRT

PΔV+VΔP=0

ΔV=-ΔPPV  ....(i)

  In second case

PΔV=-nRΔT

ΔV=-nRΔTP    ...(ii)

equating (i) and (ii)

nRΔTP=-ΔPPV

ΔT=ΔP VnR

c=vnR

Putting the value of V, n and  R , C = 150
